current_fact.fact_text: On May 5, 1945, a Japanese Fu‑Go balloon bomb—one of thousands set adrift on the jet stream—exploded near Bly, Oregon, killing six civilians on a church picnic. It remains the only lethal attack on the U.S. mainland during World War II and a haunting reminder of how far wind‑borne weapons can travel.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

current_fact.match_reason: Mysterious weapons drifting invisibly across the Pacific echo the idea of ambient, rule‑free flows that quietly travel long distances in the background.

queue.fact_text: Italian troops marched into Addis Ababa on May 5, 1936, completing Mussolini’s conquest of Ethiopia and briefly folding an ancient African empire into a fascist colonial project. The occupation sparked international outrage and deep scars, but also inspired decades of anti‑colonial resistance and Pan‑African solidarity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A painful chapter of African colonization sits alongside an episode celebrating Afrobeat, a genre that often voiced anti‑colonial and Pan‑African resistance.

queue.fact_text: On May 5, 1904, Cy Young pitched the first perfect game of Major League Baseball’s modern era, retiring all 27 Philadelphia Athletics batters without a single one reaching base. The 37‑year‑old’s feat was so precise that not one opposing hitter even managed a three‑ball count.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A rare sports 'electric' performance of flawless precision aligns with a blistering, virtuoso rock set that feels like a musical perfect game.

queue.fact_text: Mass protests erupted across Greece on May 5, 2010, as citizens confronted harsh austerity measures tied to the country’s spiraling debt crisis. Demonstrators clashed with police in central Athens, channeling anger over unemployment, wage cuts, and a sense that ordinary people were paying for systemic failures.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A society forced into painful economic lessons pairs with an episode examining struggle, frustration, and the slow, sometimes unfair process of learning and change.

queue.fact_text: Kenya Airways Flight 507 crashed shortly after takeoff from Douala, Cameroon, on May 5, 2007, killing all 114 people on board. Investigators later concluded that pilot disorientation in stormy night conditions caused the aircraft to roll and descend unnoticed until it slammed into a swamp.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A tragedy linked to overload and disorientation is gently contrasted with an episode about slowing down, regaining clarity, and moving at a safer human pace.

queue.fact_text: On May 5, 1994, the Bishkek Protocol was signed, effectively freezing active combat in the First Nagorno‑Karabakh War between Armenia and Azerbaijan. The ceasefire stopped major hostilities but left borders ambiguous and tensions unresolved, creating a fragile 'no war, no peace' that still shapes the region.

episode.title: Morning Drops #15 - Unfreezing
episode.description: A short, honest Morning Drop with no grand theme — just four tracks, a bit of voice, and the decision to not wait for the 'right' moment. Room to breathe today.
songs: Jane Birkin & Serge Gainsbourg - Je t’aime… moi non plus
Bernard Badie - In Love (DJ Quad's at Night Mix)

queue.match_reason: A ceasefire that 'freezes' a conflict matches neatly with an episode called 'Unfreezing,' hinting at the human need to move beyond suspended tensions.

queue.fact_text: Also on May 5, 1994, American teenager Michael P. Fay received four strokes of the cane in Singapore for vandalism, after intense U.S. diplomatic protests only reduced, but did not cancel, his sentence. The case sparked a global debate over corporal punishment, cultural sovereignty, and what counts as 'cruel and unusual.'

episode.title: Morning Drops #55 - JOMO
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores one of her own personality traits through the lens of psychology and music: the Joy of Missing Out. 

From the Need for Cognition to Deliberate Ignorance and Inattentional Blindness, the episode traces what it means to be someone whose brain demands depth before it gives energy, and what happens when the world around you expects the opposite.

Listen at your own pace, and let us know if you recognize yourself in this one.

Tracklist:
Burna Boy - Ye
2Pac - I Ain't Mad at Cha
Jet Life - Paper Habits
Travis Scott - Stop Trying to Be God
Andrea Laszlo De Simone - Il Regno Animale

queue.match_reason: A sensationalized punishment that everyone had an opinion on contrasts with an episode about choosing where to put your attention and embracing not joining every public drama.

queue.fact_text: Fuelling that same May 5 tapestry, the story of Jamaica’s 'discovery' in 1494 later led to a cultural crossroads where European powers, enslaved Africans, and indigenous peoples collided. Over centuries this violent mix eventually helped forge uniquely Jamaican musical forms—mento, ska, reggae—that would carry island stories worldwide.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: This wider cultural ripple from Columbus’s Caribbean landings connects with an episode tracing how colonial histories and migrations shaped modern Latin and Caribbean rhythms like reggaeton.

queue.fact_text: Saint Helena, the isolated island where Napoleon died on May 5, 1821, is also famous among scientists as one of Earth’s loneliest biodiversity labs. Because it sits so far from any continent, many species—like the now‑extinct giant earwig—evolved there and nowhere else, turning exile rock into an accidental natural experiment.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A remote Atlantic island as a living laboratory mirrors an episode that also travels to remote waters to explore unique, fragile marine worlds.

queue.fact_text: Carnegie Hall’s 1891 opening on May 5 did more than host Tchaikovsky—it helped cement New York as a global music capital. Its extraordinary acoustics came partly from guesswork: the architect had little scientific guidance and relied on proportion, wood, and intuition, yet created a hall so resonant that musicians still revere its sound.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The birth of an iconic concert hall as a musical launchpad aligns with Sedna FM’s own first episode—the place 'where it all began.'

queue.fact_text: The first continental European railway, opened between Brussels and Mechelen on May 5, 1835, was powered by British‑built locomotives named after Belgian royalty. Early passengers sat in open carriages, pelted by soot and wind, thrilled by a speed that made the countryside seem to slide rather than roll past.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A pioneering railway line that turned linear travel into a flowing landscape matches an evening mix that slowly accelerates into a cross‑continental musical journey.

published.fact_text: On May 5, 1961, Alan Shepard rode a tiny Mercury capsule named Freedom 7 on a 15‑minute suborbital hop, becoming the first American in space. Packed into a cockpit barely larger than a car seat, he reached over 187 km in altitude and splashed down in the Atlantic, proving NASA could safely send humans beyond Earth’s atmosphere.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal first leap into space pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness as creative space and shifting perspective.

published.fact_text: When Carnegie Hall opened its doors on May 5, 1891, New York christened the new Music Hall with a guest conductor from another world of sound: Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky. The Russian composer led the inaugural concerts, instantly turning the venue into a global symbol of musical prestige and transatlantic culture.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

published.match_reason: Carnegie Hall’s debut as a temple of enduring music fits an episode devoted to songs described as a 'timeless legacy.'

published.fact_text: On May 5, 1973, Secretariat didn’t just win the Kentucky Derby—he ran it so fast that no horse has matched his 1:59.40 over the classic distance since. What makes the feat even more astonishing is that he accelerated in each successive quarter‑mile, finishing with the kind of speed racehorses are supposed to lose, not gain.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A record‑shattering, accelerating race pairs with a high‑voltage rock selection meant to jolt the listener into motion.

published.fact_text: The 1930 Bago earthquake struck Myanmar (then Burma) on May 5, tearing open the ground near Bago and Yangon and killing up to 7,000 people. The quake was powerful enough to damage pagodas, topple brick buildings, and reshape how the region understood the hidden fault lines beneath its plains.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A devastating natural upheaval connects with an episode about storms, resilience, and the hope that no violent weather—literal or metaphorical—lasts forever.

published.fact_text: On his second voyage across the Atlantic, Christopher Columbus sighted Jamaica on May 5, 1494, landing at what he called Discovery Bay. To the Taíno it was already home; to Spain it became a new Caribbean claim, a lush island that would soon be swept into the currents of empire, sugar, and resistance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

published.match_reason: The European 'discovery' of Jamaica is paired with an episode steeped in reggae and dub, genres born on that same island centuries later.

published.fact_text: Napoleon Bonaparte died far from the battlefields he once dominated, on May 5, 1821, in exile on the remote island of Saint Helena. Surrounded by a small retinue, he spent his final years dictating memoirs, arguing over his legacy, and turning a wind‑scoured rock in the South Atlantic into one of history’s most famous prisons.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: Napoleon’s final exile invites a reflective mood about endings, stillness, and legacy that parallels an episode on looking back and healing.

published.fact_text: On May 5, 1877, Sitting Bull led his band of Lakota north across the border into Canada, seeking refuge from relentless U.S. Army pursuit after the Battle of the Little Bighorn. For a brief period, the prairies became a refuge from American expansion, though hunger and pressure would eventually force many to return south.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A story of an indigenous leader seeking refuge from persecution resonates with Tuareg music born from exile, resistance, and life on contested homelands.

published.fact_text: During the dramatic end to the Iranian Embassy siege in London on May 5, 1980, SAS commandos abseiled down the building’s facade and blasted their way in live on television. The operation, codenamed Nimrod, lasted just 17 minutes and turned a secretive special forces unit into a household name overnight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A high‑tension urban siege and questions of power and violence match the charged, confrontational energy of an episode centered on Rage Against the Machine.

published.fact_text: On May 5, 1940, after weeks of isolated resistance in the Norwegian campaign of World War II, the Norwegian garrison at Hegra Fortress finally surrendered to German forces. Their holdout in a snow‑covered mountain fort became a symbol of stubborn defiance in a country about to be fully occupied.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A WWII mountain fortress in Norway connects geographically and atmospherically with an episode set in Norway’s icy fjords and polar light.

published.fact_text: The Stratton Brothers case, whose trial began on May 5, 1905, was the first murder conviction in Britain based primarily on fingerprint evidence. A bloody thumbprint left on a cash box lid turned a then‑novel forensic curiosity into one of criminal investigation’s most powerful tools.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra
songs: The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind

published.match_reason: A story about noticing tiny patterns—unique fingerprints—pairs with an episode about paying deliberate, focused attention rather than scanning on autopilot.

published.fact_text: The Battle of Puebla on May 5, 1862, saw a smaller, poorly equipped Mexican force under Ignacio Zaragoza halt a much better armed French army. Though not the end of the war, the unexpected victory became a powerful symbol of resistance and is commemorated every year as Cinco de Mayo.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: Cinco de Mayo’s mix of Mexican pride, resistance, and later cross‑border celebration fits a world‑flavored, travel‑inspired set with Latin folk colors.

published.fact_text: On May 5, 1835, the first railway in continental Europe began service between Brussels and Mechelen in Belgium. Curious crowds watched steam locomotives compress distances that had taken hours by carriage into a matter of minutes, hinting that industrial speed would soon redraw the map of daily life.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: The birth of fast rail travel aligns with an 'on the move' mix built for journeys, momentum, and the thrill of starting from zero.

published.fact_text: Televised congressional hearings into the Iran–Contra affair began on May 5, 1987, pulling a secret web of arms deals and covert funding into America’s living rooms. For months, citizens watched officials testify under oath about a scandal that blurred lines between foreign policy, illegality, and plausible deniability.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

published.match_reason: A scandal that forced a nation to confront uncomfortable truths pairs with an episode about unlearning, reconsidering beliefs, and 'thinking again.'
